title: Exotic Spheres from p-adic Cohomology Theories
speaker: Jeremy Hahn
abstract: A smooth, oriented n-manifold is called a homotopy sphere if it is homeomorphic, but not necessarily diffeomorphic, to the standard n-sphere. In dimensions n>4

proof_stages.label: p-adic cohomology as K-theory input for ring spectra
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by positioning prismatic and syntomic cohomology of p-complete rings and ring spectra as computable inputs to algebraic K-theory with applications outside arithmetic geometry.

proof_stages.label: exotic-sphere p-rank growth theorem
proof_stages.summary: Homotopy spheres are organized into finite abelian groups under connected sum, and the central theorem asserts that the average p-rank of these groups tends to infinity with dimension.

proof_stages.label: Kervaire-Milnor reduction to stable stems
proof_stages.summary: The Kervaire-Milnor exact sequence reduces the asymptotic p-rank question for smooth homotopy spheres to the corresponding question for the stable homotopy groups of spheres, up to uniformly bounded cyclic errors.

proof_stages.label: stable homotopy and the first chromatic operation
proof_stages.summary: Stable homotopy is recalled as a homology theory, its mod-p version is introduced through the cofiber of multiplication by p, and the Adams operation v1 gives these groups a graded F_p[v1]-module structure.

proof_stages.label: v1-torsion and the v2-inverted target
proof_stages.summary: Miller's theorem shows that little survives after inverting v1, so the proof turns to the v1-killed theory S/(p,v1) with its v2-operation, where the main chromatic theorem is that v2^{-1}S/(p,v1) is infinite-dimensional rather than the twelve-dimensional object predicted by the telescope conjecture.

proof_stages.label: detection by the unit map into K(J_m)
proof_stages.summary: The proof studies the unit map from the sphere to the algebraic K-theory of the Adams-operation fixed-point spectrum J_m, computes the v2-inverted mod-(p,v1) target, and uses ambidexterity-produced source classes whose nonvanishing is detected by linear independence in that computable target.

proof_stages.label: bounded mod-v2 K-theory and fixed-point descent
proof_stages.summary: The p-adic input is a syntomic/prismatic computation showing that K(J_m)/(p,v1,v2) is concentrated in finitely many degrees, forcing a purity/free-module phenomenon that lets one compute before and after inverting v2; the result is obtained for ku by explicit computation and then transferred to the fixed points J_m.

proof_stages.label: geometric outlook on the syntomic stacks
proof_stages.summary: The closing perspective is that the ring-spectral constructions nevertheless give classical stacks whose geometry should be studied directly, including work in progress on Lagrangian refinements of duality analogous to known p-adic syntomic duality.

examples.name: Milnor's seven-dimensional exotic spheres
examples.note: Milnor's dimension-seven counterexamples illustrate that a homotopy sphere need not be diffeomorphic to the standard sphere before the group of homotopy spheres is introduced.
examples.engagement_reason: Milnor’s seven-dimensional exotic spheres are cited only as the famous counterexamples to standard spheres in dimension seven.

examples.name: Pinch map giving addition in homotopy groups
examples.note: Pinching the equator of a sphere into two bubbles is used to visualize the abelian group law on homotopy groups.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly traces the pinch-map construction, pinching the equator of a sphere into two bubbles and using two maps into X to define the group operation.

examples.name: Local p-adic ring K-theory analogue
examples.note: The algebraic K-theory of a p-adic ring of integers, such as a local Z_p-type example, illustrates the same finite-degree behavior after modding out by chromatic classes that underlies the purity argument.
examples.engagement_reason: The local p-adic ring K-theory case is used as an analogue and its finite-degree concentration is stated, but no concrete computation is carried out in the talk.

examples.name: Lagrangian duality refinement in syntomic cohomology
examples.note: A map from syntomic cohomology after modding out by p and v1 and inverting v2 is presented as an example of the kind of geometric structure one might expect on these stacks, parallel to known p-adic duality results.
examples.engagement_reason: The Lagrangian duality refinement in syntomic cohomology is described as a resulting work-in-progress theorem, without working through the refinement or checking it on a case.
